title: Sex-Steroid Priming Before GH Stimulation Testing
subtitle: Peripubertal Age · Testicular Volume Thresholds · CDGP False-Positive GHD

items.title: Why is sex-steroid priming used before GH stimulation testing?
items.content:
GH stimulation testing in peripubertal children with constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) can yield false-positive results — falsely low GH peaks — due to lack of physiological sex steroid priming.
Priming with sex steroids before GH stimulation is indicated when patient is peripubertal age but remains prepubertal or in early puberty, where endogenous sex steroid levels are insufficient to robustly augment pituitary GH secretion.
Short-course exogenous sex steroids mimic pubertal GH responsiveness and reduce false-positive GHD diagnoses.
keyPoints: Unprimed peripubertal prepubertal boys → falsely low GH peaks.
CDGP confounds GH stimulation interpretation.
Priming augments pituitary GH secretory response.
Reduces false-positive GHD in prepubertal peripubertal children.
items.reference: Clinical Rationale: "Growth hormone (GH) stimulation testing in peripubertal children with constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) can yield false-positive results (falsely low GH peaks) due to the lack of physiological sex steroid priming."

items.title: When is sex-steroid priming indicated before GH stimulation testing?
items.content:
Indicated: peripubertal age (e.g., ~12 years) with prepubertal exam — testicular volume <4 mL (e.g., TV 2 mL at age 12).
Not indicated: patient already pubertal — TV ≥4 mL defines onset of male puberty; endogenous testosterone sufficient to prime pituitary without exogenous steroids.
Not indicated: young child far from pubertal age (e.g., age 3, TV 1 mL) — priming not physiological; true GHD at this age evaluable without peripubertal gonadotropin confounding.
keyPoints: Prime: peripubertal age + prepubertal TV (<4 mL).
No prime: TV ≥4 mL — endogenous testosterone adequate.
No prime: toddler/young child — not peripubertal window.
TV 2 mL at age 12 = classic priming indication.
items.reference: Clinical Rationale: "Priming with sex steroids before a GH stimulation test is indicated when a patient is of peripubertal age but remains prepubertal or in early puberty, where endogenous sex steroid levels are insufficient to robustly augment pituitary GH secretion."

items.title: Why does TV 6 mL at age 12 NOT require exogenous sex-steroid priming?
items.content:
Testicular volume ≥4 mL defines onset of male puberty (gonadarche).
TV 6 mL at age 12 indicates progression well into puberty.
Endogenous testosterone levels already sufficient to prime pituitary GH secretion.
Exogenous priming is not required — would duplicate physiological pubertal priming.
keyPoints: TV ≥4 mL = puberty onset (Tanner G2).
TV 6 mL = established early puberty.
Endogenous testosterone primes pituitary.
Exogenous priming unnecessary.
items.reference: Option 1 (Incorrect): "A testicular volume (TV) of 6 mL at age 12 indicates that the patient has already progressed well into puberty (TV ≥ 4 mL defines the onset of male puberty). His endogenous testosterone levels are already sufficient to prime the pituitary, so exogenous priming is not required."

items.title: Why is age 12 with TV 2 mL the classic indication for sex-steroid priming?
items.content:
Age 12 = peripubertal chronological age when GH testing is commonly undertaken for short stature/CDGP evaluation.
TV 2 mL = completely prepubertal (below 4 mL gonadarche threshold).
Without priming, high risk of false-subnormal GH response.
Exogenous sex steroids mimic the pubertal augmentation of GH secretory capacity before provocative testing.
keyPoints: Peripubertal age + prepubertal TV = prime.
Age 12, TV 2 mL = textbook scenario.
High false-positive GHD risk without priming.
Correct answer in priming indication vignette.
items.reference: Option 2 (Correct): "At age 12, the child is at a peripubertal age, but a TV of 2 mL indicates he is completely prepubertal. Without priming, there is a high risk of a false-subnormal GH response."

items.title: Why is sex-steroid priming NOT indicated at age 3 with TV 1 mL?
items.content:
Age 3 is far from normal pubertal age.
Priming is not physiological or indicated in toddlers/young children.
GH deficiency at this age should be evaluable without confounding factor of delayed peripubertal gonadotropin activation.
The peripubertal false-positive mechanism does not apply to prepubertal toddlers.
keyPoints: Age 3 = not peripubertal window.
Priming not physiological in toddlers.
True GHD evaluable without sex steroid priming.
TV 1 mL normal for age 3 — no priming context.
items.reference: Option 3 (Incorrect): "At age 3, the child is far from the age of normal puberty. Priming is not physiological or indicated in toddlers/young children, as GH deficiency at this age should be evaluable without the confounding factor of delayed peripubertal gonadotropin activation."

items.title: How does testicular volume define the pubertal priming threshold in boys?
items.content:
Testicular volume ≥4 mL (long axis) defines onset of male puberty — gonadarche / Tanner stage G2.
Below 4 mL: prepubertal testes regardless of chronological age.
At or above 4 mL: endogenous Leydig cell testosterone production sufficient for pituitary GH priming.
TV is the key physical exam variable alongside chronological age when deciding on exogenous priming.
keyPoints: TV ≥4 mL = gonadarche/puberty onset.
TV <4 mL = prepubertal.
Combine age + TV for priming decision.
Physical exam guides priming need.
items.reference: Option 1 (Incorrect): "TV ≥ 4 mL defines the onset of male puberty" and Option 2 (Correct): "a TV of 2 mL indicates he is completely prepubertal."

items.question: When is sex-steroid priming indicated before GH stimulation testing? Select the best answer.
items.explanation: Age 12 + TV 2 mL: peripubertal age but prepubertal exam — high false-subnormal GH risk without priming. TV 6 mL and TV 15 mL: already pubertal (≥4 mL), endogenous testosterone primes pituitary. Age 3: too young — priming not physiological.
items.reference: Option 2 (Correct): "At age 12, the child is at a peripubertal age, but a TV of 2 mL indicates he is completely prepubertal. Without priming, there is a high risk of a false-subnormal GH response."

items.question: 12-year-old boy with short stature, TV 6 mL. Before GH stimulation testing, sex-steroid priming is:
options: Not required — TV ≥4 mL indicates sufficient endogenous pubertal priming
Required only if bone age is delayed
items.explanation: TV 6 mL = well into puberty. Endogenous testosterone already augments GH response. Exogenous priming unnecessary.
items.reference: Option 1 (Incorrect): "TV of 6 mL at age 12 indicates that the patient has already progressed well into puberty... endogenous testosterone levels are already sufficient to prime the pituitary, so exogenous priming is not required."

items.question: 3-year-old with growth failure, TV 1 mL. Sex-steroid priming before GH stimulation is:
options: Standard of care for all growth evaluations
Not indicated — too young; GHD evaluable without peripubertal confounding
Required because TV <4 mL at any age mandates priming
Indicated only in girls, not boys
items.explanation: Age 3 far from puberty. Priming addresses peripubertal false-low GH peaks in CDGP — not applicable to toddlers. True GHD at this age assessed without sex steroid priming.
items.reference: Option 3 (Incorrect): "At age 3, the child is far from the age of normal puberty. Priming is not physiological or indicated in toddlers/young children."

items.question: What is the primary reason for false-positive GHD on unprimed GH stimulation in peripubertal prepubertal boys?
options: Excess GH secretion from pituitary adenoma
Insufficient endogenous sex steroids to augment pituitary GH responsiveness (CDGP physiology)
items.explanation: Peripubertal chronological age without pubertal sex steroid rise (CDGP) → pituitary GH secretory capacity not fully augmented → falsely subnormal peaks on stimulation testing.
items.reference: Clinical Rationale: "GH stimulation testing in peripubertal children with constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) can yield false-positive results (falsely low GH peaks) due to the lack of physiological sex steroid priming."

items.question: Testicular volume threshold defining male puberty onset (above which endogenous priming usually suffices)?
items.explanation: TV ≥4 mL defines gonadarche/onset of male puberty. Below 4 mL = prepubertal regardless of age in priming context.
items.reference: Option 1 (Incorrect): "TV ≥ 4 mL defines the onset of male puberty."

items.question: 11-year-old boy, TV 3 mL, bone age delayed, height <3rd centile. Most appropriate pre-test preparation?
options: No preparation — proceed directly to GH stimulation
Sex-steroid priming — peripubertal age with prepubertal TV <4 mL
items.explanation: Peripubertal age + TV 3 mL (<4 mL prepubertal) = priming indicated to avoid false-subnormal GH peak. Classic CDGP/short stature workup scenario.
items.reference: Clinical Rationale: "Priming with sex steroids before a GH stimulation test is indicated when a patient is of peripubertal age but remains prepubertal or in early puberty."

items.statement: A 12-year-old boy with testicular volume 2 mL should receive sex-steroid priming before GH stimulation testing.
items.explanation: True. Peripubertal age + prepubertal TV — high risk of false-subnormal GH without priming.
items.reference: Option 2 (Correct): "At age 12, the child is at a peripubertal age, but a TV of 2 mL indicates he is completely prepubertal. Without priming, there is a high risk of a false-subnormal GH response."

items.statement: A 12-year-old boy with testicular volume 6 mL requires exogenous sex-steroid priming before GH stimulation.
items.explanation: False. TV 6 mL = pubertal. Endogenous testosterone already primes pituitary GH secretion.
items.reference: Option 1 (Incorrect): "endogenous testosterone levels are already sufficient to prime the pituitary, so exogenous priming is not required."

items.statement: Sex-steroid priming before GH stimulation is routinely indicated in 3-year-olds with TV 1 mL.
items.explanation: False. Age 3 too young. Priming not physiological in toddlers — GHD evaluable without peripubertal confounding.
items.reference: Option 3 (Incorrect): "Priming is not physiological or indicated in toddlers/young children."

items.statement: Unprimed GH stimulation in peripubertal prepubertal children can produce falsely low GH peaks mimicking GHD.
items.explanation: True. CDGP physiology — lack of sex steroid priming reduces GH responsiveness on provocative testing.
items.reference: Clinical Rationale: "can yield false-positive results (falsely low GH peaks) due to the lack of physiological sex steroid priming."

items.assertion: A 12-year-old boy with TV 2 mL should receive sex-steroid priming before GH stimulation testing.
items.reason: He is peripubertal by age but prepubertal by exam, and without priming there is high risk of a false-subnormal GH response.
items.explanation: Both true; classic priming indication — peripubertal age + TV <4 mL.
items.reference: Option 2 (Correct): "At age 12, the child is at a peripubertal age, but a TV of 2 mL indicates he is completely prepubertal. Without priming, there is a high risk of a false-subnormal GH response."

items.assertion: Exogenous sex-steroid priming is not required in a 12-year-old with TV 6 mL before GH stimulation.
items.reason: TV ≥4 mL defines puberty onset and endogenous testosterone is sufficient to prime pituitary GH secretion.
items.explanation: Both true; established puberty provides physiological priming.
items.reference: Option 1 (Incorrect): "TV ≥ 4 mL defines the onset of male puberty... endogenous testosterone levels are already sufficient to prime the pituitary, so exogenous priming is not required."
